The erotic elements from earlier chapters inform the emotional stakes here without requiring explicit continuation. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: The Road They didn't stop until dark. The road east was rougher than the way north—unpaved, little-used, winding through farmland that gradually gave way to forest. Kade drove until the horse stumbled, then drove another hour before the animal simply refused to go further. He pulled off the road into a copse of trees. Untethered the horse. Let it drink from a stream that ran through the undergrowth. Sylara hadn't spoken since Irongate. She sat in the back of the wagon where she'd started three days ago, knees drawn up, hands wrapped around herself. Not bound. There was no one to bind her now. Just the two of them and the silence and the blood drying on his shirt. He'd killed four men. Maybe five. He wasn't sure about the last one—the guard at the gate, whether the blade had caught right or just scared him. It didn't matter. Five or four, the math was the same. Murder. Theft. Breaking contract. Vashen would hear by morning. Aldric, if he'd survived, would send hunters. The bounty on them both would be substantial. He sat beside the stream and washed his hands. The water was cold, running pink, then clear. He kept scrubbing anyway. "That won't help." He looked up. Sylara stood a few feet away, arms crossed, watching him with an expression he couldn't read. "The blood," she said. "It doesn't wash off. Not really. You just learn to stop seeing it." He stood. His clothes were ruined—soaked with sweat and worse, torn at the shoulder where someone had grabbed him. He smelled like smoke and copper. "How do you know." "Varian." She said the name flatly. "The first time he made me watch. Said it was important that I understand. The second time he made me help. By the tenth time, I stopped counting." She looked at her own hands. "I still see it sometimes. In the dark. When I can't sleep." He didn't know what to say to that. So he said nothing. They made camp without a fire. Too risky. The night was cold, but they had blankets from the wagon, and her warmth beside him was enough to keep the worst of it at bay. She fell asleep quickly—exhaustion, he guessed. Or practice. She'd said once that she'd learned to sleep when she could, because opportunities were rare. He lay awake, staring up at branches barely visible against the stars. His debt was still real. Whatever happened at Aldric's house, whatever he'd done, Vashen still owned him. Three years, maybe four—and that was before today. Before he'd broken a contract, killed a collector, burned a client's house down. The price on his head would be higher than any bounty he'd ever collected. He laughed. It came out wrong, more choke than sound. Sylara stirred beside him. "What." "Nothing." "Tell me." He stared at the darkness. "Twelve years. Twelve years of taking jobs I hated, delivering people to fates I didn't want to know about, telling myself it was temporary. Just a few more deliveries. Just a few more years. And now—" He spread his hands. The gesture felt ridiculous in the dark, unseen, but he couldn't stop. "Now I'm the one with a price on my head. Hunting season's open." Another laugh, wronger than the first. "Funny, isn't it. All that time being the hunter." She was quiet for a long moment. Then she shifted closer—not touching, but near enough that he could feel her heat along his side. "My family's holdings are three weeks east," she said. "Maybe more, the way we're traveling. Slower roads. Back routes. Places hunters won't think to look." "And then what." "I don't know." Her voice was careful. "My uncle runs the house now. He's... practical. If I explain what happened—what Aldric was doing, what his son did—he might see the value in protecting me. Protecting us." "Might." "Might. Or he might decide I'm too much trouble. Sell us both to the highest bidder and pretend I was never his niece." She paused. "I told you. I don't have promises. Only chances." The stream ran somewhere nearby, audible in the silence. An owl called. Another answered. "Why did you do it?" she asked. He didn't pretend not to understand. "At the house." "You had the money. You were free—or close enough. A few more years, you said. You could have walked away. Instead you—" She stopped. He could feel her looking at him, trying to see something in the dark. "I don't know," he said. "I saw his hand on you. I saw your face. And I just—" He'd spent twelve years not thinking about the people he delivered. Not wondering what happened after. It was the only way to survive. But she'd never let him do that. She'd made him look, made him see, made him know. "Maybe I'm tired of being owned," he said. "Maybe I wanted one thing that wasn't about debt. One choice that was actually a choice." "Even if it gets you killed." "Even if." She was quiet again. Then her hand found his in the dark—not grabbing, not clinging. Just resting. Her fingers were warm, slightly callused. Real. "My sister was better at this," she said softly. "The practical parts. Surviving. She always knew which way the wind was blowing, who to trust, when to run. I was never as smart as her." "What happened to her." "Married into a trading house in the southern coast. Good match. Safe. I don't know if she's still there—if she'd remember me, or care." A breath. "I had a brother too. He died young. Fevers. My mother said he was the lucky one." "That's a hard thing to say." "My mother was a hard woman." She shifted. "She's the one who negotiated the arrangement with Aldric. A hostage for trade rights. Very practical. Very profitable. She cried when they took me away. I never knew if the tears were real." He turned his head. Her face was a shadow in the darkness, horns cutting black against the stars. "Are you afraid?" he asked. "Of what?" "Going back. Your family. Whatever waits at the end of this road." She was quiet for so long he thought she wasn't going to answer. "I'm afraid of hoping," she said finally. "I'm afraid I'll start believing something good might happen, and then it won't, and the disappointment will be worse than just expecting the worst from the start. I'm afraid I'll become my mother—practical and hard, making decisions based on what's useful instead of what's right." A pause. "I'm afraid you'll leave. That you'll wake up tomorrow and realize what you've done and decide I'm not worth it after all." Her voice cracked on the last word. Just slightly. Just enough. "I won't," he said. "You can't promise that." "No. But I can promise to try." She didn't respond. But her fingers tightened around his, and she didn't let go. They lay there in the dark, two ruined people on a back road to nowhere, both of them wanted, neither of them free. The night pressed in around them—cold, indifferent, full of things that hunted in the dark. Morning would come. The road would continue. Hunters would follow, and choices would have to be made, and nothing was certain except the weight of her hand in his and the distant promise of a family that might not remember her. He closed his eyes. Sleep came eventually—shallow, dreamless, interrupted by every sound. But when he woke, she was still there. Still warm. Still real. The horse needed water. The road needed traveling. Three weeks to the eastern reaches, if they made it that far. He stood. Offered her a hand. She took it. They drove east into the fading dark, toward something neither of them could see. --- *End.*
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter serves as the conclusion to what appears to be a well-planned fantasy erotica story, and it demonstrates several strengths while containing some notable weaknesses. The strongest aspect is character consistency and development. Both Kade and Sylara remain true to their established profiles while showing meaningful growth. Kade's decision to rescue Sylara represents a believable culmination of his character arc - a man who has been passive his entire life finally making an active choice, even at great cost. His exhaustion and self-awareness ("Twelve years of taking jobs I hated") feel authentic. Sylara's vulnerability in the darkness, admitting her fears about hoping, provides a compelling contrast to her earlier manipulative persona while remaining consistent with her established psychology. The dialogue is generally strong, avoiding exposition dumps while revealing character. Lines like "That won't help. The blood. It doesn't wash off" and "I'm afraid of hoping" feel natural and emotionally resonant. The conversation flows organically between practical concerns and deeper emotional territory. The plot coherence is solid - the chapter follows logically from the previous events and provides appropriate consequences for Kade's choice. The transition from captive/captor to fugitive partners feels earned rather than forced. However, the chapter suffers from some significant weaknesses. The prose occasionally veers into purple territory with overwrought descriptions like "two ruined people on a back road to nowhere" and "the night pressed in around them—cold, indifferent, full of things that hunted in the dark." These moments feel forced rather than organic. More problematically, the chapter relies heavily on telling rather than showing, particularly in emotional moments. Phrases like "exhaustion, he guessed" and direct statements about characters' internal states undermine the otherwise strong character work. The writing plan specifically called for avoiding emotional explanations, but the text frequently violates this principle. The ending, while thematically appropriate, feels somewhat predictable. The hand-holding in darkness and mutual vulnerability follow familiar romantic patterns without adding much novelty to the trope. The chapter does follow its plan reasonably well, covering the aftermath, the journey east, and the uncertain future. It maintains the gritty tone established earlier while allowing for character growth. Overall, this is competent writing with strong character work and dialogue, but it's held back by inconsistent prose quality and some reliance on telling over showing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 12 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 11 Amateurish: 8 Purple Prose: 10 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a substantial fantasy piece that attempts to blend bounty hunter noir with dark fantasy elements. The story follows Kade, a debt-bound bounty hunter transporting Sylara, a tiefling accused of murder, to her captor Aldric. The narrative spans eight chapters and covers their three-day journey, culminating in Kade's decision to rescue Sylara rather than complete the delivery. The character work shows genuine effort and some success. Kade is portrayed as a man trapped by inherited debt, using alcohol to cope, carrying physical and emotional scars. His gradual revelation of backstory feels organic rather than forced. Sylara is more complex - initially appearing as a manipulative prisoner who might be playing angles, she reveals genuine trauma while maintaining agency. Her filed horns serve as an effective physical manifestation of her abuse. The dynamic between them develops believably from antagonistic to intimate. The pacing is generally strong, with each chapter advancing both plot and character development. The three-day structure provides natural progression, and the author uses travel time effectively for character exploration. The village encounter in Chapter 3 provides external conflict while deepening Sylara's backstory. The intimate scene in Chapter 6 feels earned rather than gratuitous, serving character development. However, the piece suffers from several significant weaknesses. The world-building is generic fantasy with little distinctive detail. Irongate, despite being the destination, feels like a placeholder - "black stone walls against grey sky" offers nothing memorable or specific. The magic system involving tiefling blood rituals is mentioned but never developed beyond basic exposition. The dialogue often carries too much expository weight. Characters frequently explain their motivations and backstories directly rather than revealing them through action or subtext. Lines like "I killed him. Opened his throat while he was trying to explain why I should be grateful for his attention" feel more like plot summary than natural speech. The climactic action sequence at Aldric's house is rushed and lacks tactical detail. The escape feels too easy - they kill multiple guards, burn down a house, and flee a fortified city without significant resistance. This undermines the established stakes and Aldric's supposed power. The prose style is competent but unremarkable. The author avoids purple prose but also rarely achieves memorable imagery. Descriptions tend toward the functional rather than evocative. The repeated motif of Kade rubbing his palm scar works well as a character tic. The story's treatment of trauma and agency shows sensitivity, particularly in how Sylara's abuse is handled - it's acknowledged as formative without being exploitative. The theme of different forms of ownership (debt bondage vs. sexual slavery) creates meaningful parallels between the protagonists. The ending feels somewhat rushed. The transition from Kade accepting his perpetual debt to violent rebellion happens quickly, though it's adequately motivated by seeing Sylara's resignation. The final chapter's journey toward uncertain sanctuary provides appropriate ambiguity rather than false resolution. Overall, this is competent genre fiction with strong character work undermined by generic world-building and occasionally clunky exposition. The emotional core is solid, but the execution lacks the polish and distinctiveness of truly exceptional work. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 16 Pacing: 14 World Building: 10 Compelling Plot: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 11 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 12 Amateurish: 10 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 64.1/100
+ Cozy-Horror Post Apocalyptic Survival — Mushroom Season (Chapter Avg: 77.3 | Final: 86.2)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you're out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious?
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you&#x27;re out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious? -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Characters - **Protagonist options:** Former line cook/restaurateur who brings culinary expertise to survival; home gardener with plant knowledge; just an ordinary person with no special skills; a grandmother who refuses to let the apocalypse stop her from feeding people - **Companions:** A child too young to remember the before-times; an elderly neighbor with knowledge of "old ways"; a pragmatic hunter type who clashes with the cook; a small found family of 3-4 survivors in a fortified brownstone - **Antagonists:** The spore mutants themselves (fungally-infected creatures); other survivor groups who want the "recipe"; maybe no human villains at all—the horror is situational ## The Spore Mutants - Humans/animals transformed by parasitic fungus - Varying stages: shambling newly-turned vs. ancient, clever ones - Bioluminescent patches, releasing spore clouds - Make clicking/growling sounds, move in packs - **The horror:** They might retain some memory of being human; they seem to learn and adapt; they have a hive mind - **The culinary angle:** The fungal fruiting bodies on them are technically edible—like grotesque chicken of the woods mushrooms; the meat beneath is tinged with umami from the infection ## Plot Directions 1. **Discovery → Exploitation → Consequence:** Starving survivors discover the mutants are edible, build a new food system, then face consequences (mutants adapt, spores affect eaters, ethical reckoning) 2. **The Gourmet Apocalypse:** Dark comedy angle—survivors become monster foodies, developing recipes, arguing about preparation 3. **Body Horror Transformation:** Eating them slowly changes the survivors; they become something new 4. **Reversal of Power:** Mutants begin fearing humans; humans become the monsters 5. **The Communion:** The spores connect survivors to the hive mind; they learn the mutants' tragic origin ## Tone Approaches - **Cozy emphasis:** Warm cooking scenes, found family bonding, creating home amid chaos, dark humor, sensual food descriptions - **Horror emphasis:** Gore of butchery, body horror, the uncanny valley of cooking humanoid creatures, mounting dread - **The sweet spot:** Domestic warmth undercut by existential wrongness; comfort that feels slightly perverse; humor that gallows-slides into genuine horror ## Story Beats to Consider - The desperate first bite (accidental? deliberate?) - First successful hunt and the adrenaline/terror - The cooking montage—making something delicious from something monstrous - Sharing the meal with others (revulsion → hunger → acceptance) - Building systems: hunting parties, butchering stations, recipe development - Small cozy moments: board games by firelight, gossip over stew, birthday celebrations - The mutants begin behaving differently (avoiding them? organizing?) - A character gets infected—but eating the fungus might slow it? - Discovery of what the mutants were/are - Final equilibrium: acceptance of the new normal, comfortable unease ## Settings - Fortified brownstone with rooftop garden - Rural farmhouse with root cellar - Converted warehouse with commercial kitchen - Underground bunker ## Potential Endings - **Dark comfort:** They've found sustainable food, but they've lost something essentially human - **Hopeful horror:** The connection to the hive mind lets them coexist; the mutants avoid them now - **Cyclic dread:** They realize other survivors are hunting *them* now - **Quiet acceptance:** A shared meal, warmth, and the unspoken knowledge that they're slowly changing --- # Reflection **What's working:** The **former chef protagonist** is compelling because it gives a legitimate reason for the culinary experimentation and provides a unique voice—someone who thinks in terms of flavor profiles, texture, and technique even when faced with monstrous ingredients. This character would legitimately be excited about a new ingredient, which creates interesting psychological tension. A **small found family** (4-5 people max) works better for 8,000 words than a larger community. This allows for deeper character work and maintains intimacy. The cozy element comes from their domestic routines and relationships. The **urban brownstone** setting provides natural defenses, a sense of home, and opportunities for rooftop gardening (showing they tried normal food sources first). It's intimate and can feel warm despite the danger outside. **Body horror transformation** is tempting but might overwhelm the cozy element. A lighter touch—perhaps the spores create a *connection* rather than a transformation—preserves the cozy-horror balance. **The reversal of power** (mutants learning to fear humans) is a strong mid-to-late story beat that adds horror without removing comfort. It's unsettling without being traumatizing. **What's not working:** Pure **dark comedy** risks undercutting both the genuine coziness and the genuine horror. The humor should emerge naturally from situations, not drive the tone. Having **human antagonist** survivors feels unnecessary—the situation itself is antagonist enough, and other humans would split focus. **Complete transformation** of characters into mutants is too tragic for cozy-horror. The ending needs unease, not despair. **The sweet spot:** The story should feel like a warm meal in a dark room. The comfort is real, the danger outside is real, and there's a persistent wrongness that's become normalized. The horror isn't that everything goes wrong—it's that everything goes *strangely right*, and that's almost worse. The protagonist should be someone who genuinely finds joy in cooking and feeding others—this makes the cozy elements authentic and makes the horror elements (butchering humanoid creatures, the ethics of eating sentience-adjacent beings) more impactful by contrast. For the 8-chapter structure, I'm thinking: two chapters of desperate starvation leading to discovery, three chapters of building the "new normal" with cozy cooking and found family moments, then three chapters of escalating wrongness leading to an equilibrium that's comfortable but unsettling.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Protagonist:** Mari, formerly a line cook at a farm-to-table restaurant. She's in her early thirties, practical, and finds genuine joy in feeding people. Her identity was built around nourishing others—the apocalypse took that from her, and discovering she can cook again gives her purpose. She's not reckless or darkly comedic; she's desperately earnest. **Found Family:** Four survivors total in a fortified Chicago brownstone: - Mari (protagonist, cook) - Dante (early 40s, former handyman, handles security and repair) - Wren (eight years old, remembers nothing of the before-times) - Gert (70s, a retired mycologist who initially identifies the fungal growths as potentially edible) **Setting:** An urban brownstone in a spore-fogged city. The upper floors are living quarters; the roof has failed garden beds; the basement is a root cellar and now a butchering space. The interior is warm—candles, blankets, a working fireplace—creating intentional coziness against the outside threat. **Plot Arc:** The story follows a clear emotional trajectory: desperate hunger leads to accidental discovery, which leads to the complicated joy of cooking again, which leads to the slow realization that eating the mutants creates a spore-bond—a low-level hive-mind connection. This connection isn't traumatizing; it's *comforting*, which is the horror. The mutants begin avoiding them, seemingly recognizing them as "part of the network." The story ends with a new equilibrium: they're safe, fed, and slowly, comfortably changing. **Tone & Voice:** Third-person limited from Mari's perspective. The prose will be grounded and sensory, lingering on food descriptions with the same attention whether she's describing a perfect stock or the butchering of a mutant. The horror is understated—a wrongness that accumulates rather than shocks. Cozy moments are genuinely warm, not ironic. Humor emerges naturally from character dynamics, not from the absurdity of the situation. **Stylistic Aims:** - Sensuous food writing that makes the reader hungry, then uncomfortable about being hungry - Domestic warmth that never winks at the camera - Horror through accumulation and implication rather than visceral gore (though butchery will be clinical and unflinching) - Slow pacing that allows routine to become comforting before it becomes disturbing **What to Avoid:** - Dark comedy that undercuts genuine emotion - Human antagonists or conflict with other survivor groups - Complete transformation or tragedy - Explaining too much about the mutants' origins or the science - Making the characters callous or unbothered by what they're doing --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: The Last Inventory Mari conducts a careful inventory of their remaining food: three cans of beans, half a bag of rice, some withered root vegetables from the failed roof garden. We meet the household—Dante boarding up a weakened window, Gert sorting through her reference books, Wren coloring by candlelight. The warmth of their home contrasts with the foggy, dangerous world outside. Mari remembers her life as a cook, the satisfaction of a busy service, the particular joy of feeding strangers. The chapter ends with Gert casually mentioning that the fungal fruiting bodies on the mutants resemble edible varieties she studied—half a joke, half a desperate observation. No one laughs. No one dismisses it either. ## Chapter 2: The Hunt Starvation forces a decision. Mari and Dante go on a hunt—they've avoided mutants before, but now they need to get close. The experience is terrifying: the fog, the clicking sounds, the bioluminescent patches moving in the dark. They find a smaller, solitary mutant and kill it with makeshift weapons. The violence is ugly and exhausting. They drag it home, shaking. The chapter ends with Mari staring at the body in the basement, a cleaver in her hand, trying to see it as food instead of a monster. ## Chapter 3: The First Meal Mari butchers the mutant with professional detachment—the anatomy is wrong but recognizable. The fungal growths are dense, moist, surprisingly fragrant. She cooks simply: seared meat, roasted fungal caps, a broth from the bones. The smell fills the house for the first time in months. The group gathers to eat, uncertain and afraid, but hunger wins. The first bite is the hardest. Then it's just... good. The chapter ends with the four of them sitting around the fire, full and warm, not talking about what they just did. ## Chapter 4: Kitchen Work A week later. Mari has developed a system: hunting parties, butchering protocols, preparation techniques. She treats the work with the same seriousness she gave her restaurant kitchen. The chapter follows a full day of domestic routine—Gert preserving meat, Dante mending clothes, Wren helping Mari knead bread from ground acorns. They tell stories, joke, argue about recipes. A genuine sense of home and comfort develops. But Mari notices something: when she handles the raw meat, she feels a faint hum, a static charge at the base of her skull. ## Chapter 5: The Edge of Something The hum grows stronger for Mari. She doesn't tell the others, but she finds herself pausing during meal prep, lost in a sensation like almost-remembering. Meanwhile, the hunting patterns change—the mutants seem to be avoiding them. A planned hunt yields nothing; the streets are eerily empty. Gert notices the bioluminescent patches on a distant mutant pulse in rhythm with Mari's movements. The chapter ends with Mari reaching out to touch a fungal growth on a fresh kill and feeling something reach back—a presence, curious and distant. ## Chapter 6: Communion Mari experiments alone. She prepares a meal with specific attention—good technique, careful seasoning, intentional eating. When she eats, the connection opens wider: fragmentary images, emotions, a sense of vastness. She sees the mutants not as monsters but as something that was human, still is human in a way, transformed but not gone. She understands that eating them has woven her into their network. She's horrified. She's also less lonely than she's been in two years. She doesn't tell the others the full truth, but she encourages them to eat with her, to really taste. ## Chapter 7: The Offering A hunt goes strangely. The mutants don't flee or fight—they part, making a path. One approaches Dante and Mari without aggression, standing still, almost presenting itself. It's old, covered in thick fungal plates, and its eyes hold something like recognition. Dante can't do it. Mari can. She kills it quickly, mercifully, feeling the network shiver with something like gratitude. The chapter is quiet and disturbing. Back home, she prepares the meal with extra care. The others eat, and Mari watches their faces, wondering if they feel it too—if they're all becoming part of something larger. ## Chapter 8: New Equilibrium Three months later. Life is good. The mutants give them a wide berth now; hunting is almost too easy. Mari cooks elaborate meals, invents new recipes, takes pride in her work again. The household is healthy, warm, and close. But small details accumulate: Gert's occasional confusion about whether a thought was hers; Dante's skin showing faint luminescent patches; Wren knowing things she couldn't know. In the final scene, Mari examines her own forearm in candlelight, watching the faint glow pulse beneath her skin. She feels the network humming at the edge of her mind—vast, patient, welcoming. She turns back to the stove, stirs the stew, and calls everyone for dinner. They gather around the table, and it's warm and good, and they're all slowly becoming something else together. The last line: "Eat," Mari said, and they did.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
## How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer would likely bring a specific, idiosyncratic obsession to this premise. They'd fixate on something personal—maybe they hunted with their father, maybe they've experienced real hunger, maybe they've watched someone they love transform into someone unrecognizable. The story would circle that obsession compulsively, returning to it in ways that might not even be intentional. A human would also probably be weirder about the food. Not "impressive food writing" but specific textures, smells, and associations that are deeply personal—maybe even repellent to others but meaningful to them. A human would also likely be messier with structure. They might spend three pages on something that seemed irrelevant, or rush past a moment that feels important. There'd be accidents in the text—things that don't quite fit but are more interesting because of it. The cozy moments might feel more earned because they'd emerge from genuine weariness rather than narrative allocation. Most importantly, a human would probably be less certain about what the story means. They might not know if eating the mutants is tragic, hopeful, or just what survival looks like. That uncertainty would bleed into the prose—not as ambiguity-for-ambiguity's-sake, but as genuine artistic exploration. --- ## Common LLM Tendencies & How They Might Fail This Piece **1. Thematic Consistency and Clarity** LLMs tend to make themes explicit and consistent. The horror-cozy tension would be clearly signaled and evenly distributed. Every cozy moment would have its horror counterpoint, creating a predictable rhythm. *How this fails the piece:* Real unease comes from moments where you're not sure how to feel. A human writer might let the cozy scenes be genuinely, uncomplicatedly warm—making the reader feel safe before pulling the rug. If every scene announces its tonal tension, there's no tension. **2. Character Functionalism** LLMs tend to create characters who serve narrative functions: Gert exists to provide mycological knowledge, Dante to handle security, Wren to represent innocence and raise the stakes. Each character has their lane. *How this fails the piece:* Real people are more redundant and contradictory. Gert might be most valuable for her stories, not her knowledge. Dante might be terrible at security but good for morale. Wren might be annoying or strange, not just endearing. The functional approach makes the found family feel constructed rather than accidental. **3. Symmetrical Structure** The eight-chapter plan is very balanced: two chapters of hunger, three of building comfort, three of escalating wrongness. Each chapter has its assigned emotional job. *How this fails the piece:* Life isn't symmetrical. A human writer might linger too long in the cooking scenes because they're enjoying them, or rush through a hunt because it's unpleasant to write. These imbalances often make work feel more alive, not less. **4. Explaining the Horror** The "network" concept is clean and explainable. The mutants have a hive mind, eating them connects you to it, connection spreads—mechanical clarity. *How this fails the piece:* The best cosmic horror is genuinely inexplicable—not "mysterious but ultimately comprehensible." A human might not fully understand what they're describing, and that confusion would be more unsettling than any clear system. **5. The Neat Ending** The ending has everyone changing together, in equilibrium, with a clean final line that encapsulates the theme. It's a satisfying conclusion. *How this fails the piece:* Real endings often feel like stopping rather than concluding. A human might end on an image that doesn't explain itself, or a moment that raises more questions than it answers. The "they're all becoming something together" ending might be too comfortable—too much like a resolution. --- ## Deep Critique of the Current Plan **The Convenience Problem** The group is too complete. A mycologist, a cook, a handyman, and a child—everyone has a role, everyone contributes. It's a balanced RPG party. Real found families are messier. You end up with two people who can't do anything useful and one person doing all the work. You end up with resentment, with dead weight that you can't cut loose because they're human. *Mari's relationship to cooking might be too clean:* She was a cook, she loses cooking, she finds cooking again in monster meat. What if she hated her restaurant job? What if she has complicated feelings about feeding people—the obligation of it, the way it made her feel needed and trapped? The discovery of mutant meat might not be a return to joy but a new, stranger relationship with food entirely. **The Sanitized Body Horror** The butchering and cooking scenes risk being too aesthetically pleasing—"food writing but make it monstrous." A human would probably be more honest about the gross parts: the smell that lingers, the texture of the fungal plates, the way the meat resists the knife in places, gives too easily in others. The mundane physical realities of butchering something humanoid. *What's missing:* The psychological weight of recognizing anatomy. The first time Mari cuts into something that looks like a human hand. The way she might dissociate, compartmentalize, or develop rituals to handle it. The plan mentions "professional detachment" but that's the outcome—what's the process of building that detachment? **The Underdeveloped Relationships** The plan shows the group getting along, cooking together, telling stories—but what's the friction? Who irritates whom? Who has different ideas about how to survive? What does Mari think about when she looks at Gert vs. Dante vs. Wren? *Wren specifically:* The child feels like emotional decoration—there to be innocent, to raise stakes, to make the survival feel more meaningful. But children are strange. They say unsettling things. They notice what adults ignore. Wren might be the first to notice something wrong, or the most accepting of the new food, or the one who asks the question no one wants to answer. She should have more agency, more weirdness. **The Missing Guilt** The characters seem to move past the horror too quickly. Chapter 3 has them eating, uncomfortable but eating. By Chapter 4, they're comfortable. But real guilt doesn't work that way. It recurs. It surfaces at odd times. Mari might find herself unable to eat what she's cooked, or eating compulsively to avoid thinking about it. *What's missing:* The rationalization, the denial, the ways humans lie to themselves. Dante might tell himself they have no choice, over and over, until he believes it. Gert might intellectualize it—classify it as "just biology." Mari might throw herself into the work of cooking to avoid the meaning of the work. **The Too-Clean Horror System** The "network" and "hive mind" concepts are too sci-fi, too comprehensible. This risks turning cosmic horror into a magic system—with rules, mechanisms, predictable effects. *Alternative:* What if the connection is genuinely unclear? Mari feels something, but is it real? Is she losing her mind? Do the others feel it too, or are they just going along with her strangeness because she's feeding them? The uncertainty is scarier than the system. **The Ending Is Too Resolved** "All becoming something together" is almost comforting—communal transformation, no one alone. But what if one of them isn't changing? What if Gert is dying from the spores while the others thrive? What if Wren is changing faster, differently? What if Mari can feel the network but the others can't—or claims she can't? The final line ("Eat," Mari said, and they did) is strong but complete. It closes the story. A more human ending might be more open—an image, a question, a moment that continues past the page. --- ## Brainstorming: Making It More Human **Character Complications:** - What if Mari wasn't a good cook? What if she was a line cook who hated her job, resented the industry, and cooking for the group is the first time she's felt ownership over her work—but the ownership is tainted by what she's cooking? - What if Dante was a coward who abandoned someone? His competence at security is overcompensation. He volunteers for hunts to prove himself, but he's terrified. - What if Gert's knowledge is incomplete, maybe dangerously wrong? She says the fungus is edible based on old books, but she's not certain. She's gambling with all their lives and hiding her doubt. - What if Wren is strange in a way that's not explainable as trauma? She says things that feel too old for her. She stares at the mutants too long. She might already be connected to something. **Structural Imbalances:** - Let Chapter 4 be almost entirely cozy—no horror counterpoint, just genuine warmth. Make the reader forget to be worried. - Put a significant interpersonal conflict in Chapter 5 or 6 that has nothing to do with the mutants—old resentments, different values, a fight about something small that reveals something large. - Let a hunt fail completely. Let them come home empty-handed, ashamed, angry at each other. The mutants are unpredictable; the survivors aren't competent every time. **Messier Horror:** - Mari discovers that the mutants are more human than she thought—finds a photo in a mutant's pocket, or a wedding ring fused into fungal flesh. The recognition can't be undone. - One of the mutants during a hunt behaves in a way that seems like pleading or recognition. Mari kills it anyway. She can't stop thinking about it. - The food itself becomes a character. Mari develops a signature dish. The others ask for it by name. The normalization is the horror. **Ambiguity Over Clarity:** - The "network" is never confirmed. Mari feels things, sees things, but is she connecting to a hive mind or is she slowly losing her grip on reality from trauma and spore exposure? Let the reader genuinely not know. - Don't explain why the mutants start avoiding them. Maybe it's the network. Maybe it's that the group has become more dangerous. Maybe it's coincidence. Let multiple interpretations coexist. **A Less Resolved Ending:** - End on a moment of genuine uncertainty—maybe Mari looks at Wren and can't tell if the girl's eyes briefly luminesced, or if it was just the firelight. The reader doesn't get confirmation. - Or end on a moment of ordinary tenderness that's been contaminated—Wren hugs Mari, and Mari feels the hum in her bones, and it feels like love. Is it love? Is it the network? Is there a difference anymore? Don't answer. **Specific Human Details to Include:** - The way Mari's hands smell after butchering, no matter how much she washes them - A recipe that Gert remembers from her mother that they can't make anymore—missing ingredients—but Mari tries to approximate it with mutant meat anyway - Wren's specific, strange questions: "Did the monsters have names?" "Do they have families?" "Are we eating someone's dad?" - Dante's ritual before a hunt—something small, superstitious, never explained - The games they play, the songs they sing, the shows they miss—the cultural debris of the before-times, worked into their new life - A moment where Mari cooks something perfectly and feels genuine pride, then feels sick about feeling pride
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Protagonist:** Mari, 33, was a line cook who hated her job—the long hours, the thankless pace, the way feeding strangers felt more like assembly line work than care. She wasn't good at it either; she was adequate, and adequacy became its own trap. Now, cooking for this small group is the first time she's felt ownership over her work, but the ownership is complicated by what she's cooking. She's not returning to joy—she's discovering a stranger, more desperate relationship with food. **The Found Family (messier, more friction):** - **Gert (72)** was a hobbyist mycologist, not a professional—her knowledge comes from books and weekends, and she's gambling that it applies to infected fungal growths. She doesn't advertise her uncertainty. - **Dante (41)** was a convenience store manager who abandoned his wife during the first outbreak. He handles security because he volunteers for everything, desperate to prove he's not a coward. He is, by his own private definition, a coward. - **Wren (8)** says things that are too old for her, stares at the fog too long, and was found wandering alone six months ago. She doesn't remember the before-times, or claims she doesn't. She is strange in ways that aren't explainable as trauma. **Setting:** A Chicago brownstone, fortified over two years. The neighborhood is quiet now—most survivors fled or died in the first year. The fog rolls in every evening, and the mutants come with it. The house is genuinely warm: a working fireplace, salvaged furniture, walls covered in Wren's drawings. It feels like a home because they've made it one, deliberately, stubbornly. **The Horror Approach:** The "network" or "hive mind" is never confirmed. Mari begins to feel things—a hum, a presence, fragmentary impressions—but the story never verifies whether this is a genuine connection or a psychological break caused by trauma, starvation, and possible spore exposure. Multiple interpretations must coexist. The mutants begin avoiding them, but is it recognition, fear, or coincidence? No answers. **The Cozy Approach:** Domestic warmth is real, uncomplicated, and earned. The cooking scenes are genuinely satisfying—the pleasure of a good meal after hunger, the comfort of routine, the intimacy of feeding people you love. The horror isn't in cozy moments being fake; it's in cozy moments being real while something else accrues unnoticed. **Voice & Style:** Third-person limited from Mari's perspective, with close psychic distance. Prose is grounded in physical sensation—the texture of things, the smell, the weight. Food writing is sensuous and detailed, whether describing the pleasure of eating or the grotesquerie of butchering. No irony, no winking. Emotional beats are underplayed rather than announced. **What to Avoid:** - Explaining the network or confirming its existence - Characters who function only as narrative tools - Symmetrical distribution of cozy/horror beats - Resolved endings or thematic closure - Making the characters more competent or heroic than they are - Sanitized violence—the butchering is detailed, difficult, and leaves residue --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: Inventory Mari counts what's left: eleven cans, half a bag of rice, some withered potatoes that never grew right. The ritual of counting is familiar, almost soothing—she used to do inventory at the restaurant, ticking off items on a clipboard, feeling nothing. Now she crouches in the basement with a flashlight, and the numbers make her chest tight. We see the brownstone: the fortified windows, the fireplace they gather around, Wren's drawings tacked to the walls. Dante comes in from a perimeter check, empty-handed. Gert is rereading a field guide for the dozenth time, and Mari catches her muttering "should work, it should work" to herself—not confident, just desperate enough to believe. The chapter ends with Gert saying aloud what they've all been avoiding: the fungal growths on the mutants might be edible. The word "might" hangs in the air. No one argues. ## Chapter 2: The First Hunt Three days later. One can of beans left. Mari and Dante go out together because neither will say no and neither wants to go alone. The hunt is clumsy and terrifying—they're not hunters, just hungry people with sharpened tools. They find a solitary mutant in an alley, smaller than most. Dante freezes at the crucial moment. Mari doesn't—she swings a rebar pipe, again and again, until it stops moving. She's never killed anything before. Dante retches in the fog but helps her drag the body home. They don't speak. The chapter is short and brutal, ending with the body in the basement and Mari shaking so hard she can't hold a glass of water steady. ## Chapter 3: Butchery Mari goes down to the basement alone. She tells herself to see it as meat, but the anatomy is wrong in ways she can't ignore—the joint of a shoulder, the grace note of a wrist bone, the vague suggestion of a face she refuses to look at. Her training kicks in: assess the cuts, find the usable parts. The fungal growths are dense and wet, and she makes herself handle them, cut them free. Gert has told her which parts might be safest, based on books written for a world that no longer exists. The work takes hours. Mari's hands shake. She dissociates at one point, finding herself standing still, staring, unable to remember the last five minutes. She washes three times afterward. The smell doesn't leave. ## Chapter 4: The Meal Mari cooks simply at first—seared meat, roasted fungal caps—but the instinct takes over. She makes a stock. She seasons carefully, tastes and adjusts, falls into the rhythm of doing the job right even when the job is wrong. The smell fills the house, and it smells like food, like real food, like something she hasn't made in two years. When she calls the others down, they gather around the table and stare. Wren is the first to take a bite. She chews, considers, and says it tastes like "the before-times," which makes no sense because she doesn't remember them. Then Dante eats. Then Gert. Then Mari. It's good. That's the worst part—it's genuinely, Warmly good. They eat until the pot is empty. No one mentions what they're eating. After, they sit by the fire, and Wren hums a song no one recognizes, and for a little while, the house feels safe. ## Chapter 5: The New Routine Two weeks of full bellies. The chapter follows a single ordinary day: Mari inventing a stew recipe, Gert teaching Wren to read from old seed catalogs, Dante reinforcing the basement shutters. The warmth is real and unforced—jokes, stories, an argument about whether Gert's grandmother's biscuit recipe could be adapted for acorn flour that ends in laughter. Mari feels useful for the first time in years. She notices small things: her hands don't shake anymore; she sleeps through the night; the food satisfies something deeper than hunger. There is no horror counterpoint in this chapter. Just comfort. Just the strange, precious ordinary. ## Chapter 6: The Hum Another hunt. This one goes easier—Mari is faster, more precise, less afraid. But butchering the body, she feels something new: a hum at the base of her skull, like a tuning fork struck somewhere far away. She pauses, meat in her hands, and listens. It fades. She tells herself it's exhaustion. At dinner that night, she watches Wren more carefully, noticing that the girl stares out the window toward the fog sometimes, her lips moving without sound. When Mari asks what she's saying, Wren smiles and says, "Just listening." That night, Mari lies awake, and the hum returns—fainter, almost friendly. She doesn't sleep. She doesn't tell anyone. ## Chapter 7: Failure A hunt goes wrong. The mutants they find aren't scattering like they have been; they're standing still, watching. Mari and Dante retreat, empty-handed. The failure cracks something open. Back home, with hunger returning and rationing resumed, Dante snaps at Gert about the seed catalog lessons, then at Wren for humming, then at Mari for the tasteless stew made from their last stored portions. It's not about the food—it's about his wife, how he closed a door behind him and ran, how he volunteers for every hunt trying to be someone who wouldn't run. He says some of this out loud, in pieces, between the shouting. Mari listens. She realizes she's felt the hum all day, stronger than before, and it's angry—or she is—and she can't tell the difference anymore. No one eats well that night. ## Chapter 8: Acceptance The morning after the fight, Mari goes out alone. She finds a mutant waiting at the end of the block—standing still, almost patient, its bioluminescent patches pulsing slowly in the fog. She approaches it. The hum is loud now, filling her skull, and she recognizes something in it: not a voice, not words, but a feeling like recognition, like being seen. She kills the mutant quickly. She thanks it. She doesn't know if she means it. Back home, she prepares the meat with care and serves it that evening. Everyone eats. After dinner, Gert mentions her hands have been aching less. Dante rolls up his sleeves to show faint pale patches on his forearms, luminescent in the firelight—they all examine them with curiosity rather than fear. Wren is already glowing faintly at the temples, has been for weeks, no one mentions it. Mari looks at her family around the table, full and warm, and feels the hum settle into something like contentment. Whether she's connected to something larger or slowly losing her mind, she can't say. It doesn't matter anymore. She's never been less alone. The story ends with Wren asking for seconds, and Mari rising to get them, and the fire burning low, and the fog pressing gentle against the windows.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mari **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Mari was a line cook at a farm-to-table restaurant in Chicago for six years. She hated it—the burns, the heat, the customers she never saw, the head chef who said "family" but meant "unpaid overtime." She stayed because she was adequate, and adequacy was a comfortable trap. Her parents live (lived?) in Michigan; she called once a month and lied about being happy. She had a boyfriend who left her for a coworker two years before the outbreak, and she was still parsing that grief when the world ended. She's practical—not heroically, just stubbornly. She keeps going because stopping feels like more effort. She compartmentalizes aggressively: the thing she butchers and the thing she cooks live in separate boxes, and she doesn't open them at the same time. She's bad at comfort, offers solutions when people need warmth. She resents being needed and needs to be needed, a contradiction she doesn't examine. **Idiosyncrasies:** Counts things when anxious (cans, steps, breaths). Hates raw tomatoes. Talks to herself while cooking. Can't sleep without weight on her feet—a blanket tucked tight. Remembers recipes in fragments, fills gaps with invention. Touches the scar on her chin when thinking. **Flaws:** Cowardly in the ways that matter—avoids hard conversations, won't admit she's wrong. Judges people constantly and silently. Gets irritable when scared. Has never apologized to Dante for what she thinks about him. Suspects, in her worst moments, that she prefers the apocalypse to the before-times, and buries this so deep it might as well not exist. **Physicality:** Average height, strong forearms and hands from kitchen work. Brown hair cut badly with kitchen shears. Moves economically—aware of her body's dimensions. Service-industry stare: present but distant. Hands rough, nails bitten short, a crescent-shaped burn scar on her left forearm. Smells permanently of woodsmoke and onions. Expressions subtle; her mood lives in her shoulders. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Wants to feel useful, to not be hungry, to not think too hard. Wants to protect Wren but isn't sure she's suited for it. Wants to not be alone, which she'd never admit. Her loyalty to the group might be love or might be proximity—she doesn't trust herself to know the difference. *Dante:* She sees her own cowardice reflected in him and resents him for it. They work well together but avoid real talk. She's harder on him than she should be. *Gert:* Respects her, worries about her age. Gert is the closest thing to a parent in the house, which Mari would never acknowledge needing. *Wren:* Complicated. Feels responsible but not maternal. Finds Wren's strangeness unsettling and validating—it's okay to be strange here. **Voice Examples:** *Internal:* "Three cans of tomatoes. Two of beans. One without a label—peaches or dog food, impossible to say. Counting helped. Counting was a shape." *Dialogue:* "Gert. I need you to be honest. Not hopeful. Honest. Can we eat this or not?" "It's just meat. It's just—it's just meat. Say it until it's true." --- # Dante **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Managed a 7-Eleven for eleven years. He kept showing up, kept being reliable, and eventually they gave him keys. He had a wife, Elena. They married young and stayed married through inertia. No children, though they tried, then stopped trying, then stopped talking about it. During the first outbreak, he and Elena were separated in a crowd. He saw a door and took it—closed it behind him and kept running. He doesn't know if she's alive. He's never told anyone. He tells himself the door swung shut on its own, and the lie is so practiced it almost feels true. He wants to be useful, to be seen as someone who stays. He volunteers for everything. Craves approval desperately. Performs bravery badly, like an actor who hasn't rehearsed. Falls back on customer-service cheerfulness at the worst moments. **Idiosyncrasies:** Whistles tunelessly when nervous. Checks locks three times before bed. Organizes things by size when stressed. Weak stomach for gore but forces himself to look. Remembers everyone's birthday. Still talks about "when things get back to normal." **Flaws:** Runs from confrontation and shame. Lies to himself expertly. Takes criticism as proof he's hated. Overcompensates constantly. Resents Wren sometimes for being young enough to adapt. Jealous of Mari's competence. **Physicality:** Tall, going soft in the middle before the apocalypse hardened him. Losing hair, now shaves his head. Kind face, open in a way that reads trustworthy or naive. Expressive eyes—he never learned to hide what he's feeling. Hands that don't know what to do with themselves. Walks like he's apologizing for taking up space. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Wants to be the kind of man who didn't run. Wants forgiveness for something no one knows he did. Wants to matter, to be essential. *Mari:* Respects her competence, feels judged (he is), wants her approval badly. Thinks she's stronger than him. He's right. *Gert:* Genuinely fond. Sees her as grandmother and feels protective—partly real, partly performance. *Wren:* The child he never had, which means she's also a reminder. Plays games, tells stories, sometimes looks at her with something like grief. **Voice Examples:** *Internal:* "He told himself the door swung shut on its own. The memory wore smooth, a stone in a river. He could almost believe it. Almost." *Dialogue:* "Coffee's ready. It's brown and warm, that's something, right?" "I've got the next watch. I slept plenty. I'm good." "Wren, want to learn knots? Uncle Dante knows at least three. Maybe four. Okay, two. Two good knots." --- # Gert **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Librarian for forty years. The mushroom hobby started after her husband died—a reason to leave the house, join foraging groups, fill notebooks. She's not an expert. She knows enough to know how much she doesn't know. When the world ended, she grabbed her books. Now she's the closest thing to an authority they have. Calm in the way of someone who's already survived her worst fear. Not fearless, just exhausted by panic. Pragmatic, dry-humored. Has a scientist's caution and a grandmother's warmth, which conflict more often than they should. Her certainty is performance—confidence for the others' sake. She loves quietly but completely. **Idiosyncrasies:** Talks to her books, apologizes when she drops one. Eats slowly. Keeps mental catalog of everyone's preferences. Hums hymns while working. Has a shoebox of "beautiful things"—a stone, a marble, a feather. Calls mutants "the changed ones," never "monsters." **Flaws:** Her certainty-mask fools herself sometimes. Intellectualizes to avoid feeling. Has privately decided she'll die soon anyway, takes risks she shouldn't. Treats people like research subjects. Doesn't talk about feelings. Underestimates how much they need her. **Physicality:** Short, round, weathered hands with arthritic knuckles she rubs when thinking. White hair cut practical. Moves slowly but with intention. Layers cardigans over flannels—always cold. Reading glasses always lost and found. Laughs with her whole body. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Wants to matter, even at the end. Wants her knowledge to be useful, worth something. Wants to protect these people—not because they're family but because they're people. Wants to not die messily, suspects she won't get to choose. *Mari:* Easy respect. Sees her competence and her struggle, doesn't push. *Dante:* Patient with his performance, sees through it, loves him anyway. He reminds her of her son, who she hasn't spoken to in fifteen years. *Wren:* Delight and unease in equal measure. Teaches her to read because someone should. **Voice Examples:** *Internal:* "The field guide said 'choice edible,' but it was written before the fungus grew on people. 'Should be fine' was not 'will be fine.' She said it anyway." *Dialogue:* "Hmm. Bring it closer. Light's terrible—everyone's in everyone's shadow." "The changed ones aren't monsters, Dante. They're people who caught something. We could have caught it. Might still. Pass the salt." "I'm old enough to remember when 'mushrooms for dinner' was a treat. Funny world." --- # Wren **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Found wandering alone six months into the outbreak, in a house with three dead bodies. No identification, no memory of her name—Dante called her Wren because she was small and loud. She says she doesn't remember before, but sometimes says things like "like the car we had" and then looks confused when asked. She's eight: bright, curious, easily delighted, difficult to rattle. Treats the apocalypse as normal because it's all she clearly remembers. Asks questions too insightful or too strange. Sometimes stares at things that aren't there, hums songs no one recognizes, says things that feel like non-sequiturs until later. She's not haunted—she's porous. Things move through her. **Idiosyncrasies:** Hums constantly, fragments of melody with no name. Flinches at unexpected touch, then apologizes. Draws the same image repeatedly: house, tree, sun. Talks to furniture, says "excuse me" when she bumps into it. Eats everything put in front of her like she's making up for hunger she can't remember. Knows things she shouldn't—asked Gert once if her son was "still alive in Michigan." Gert had never told anyone where her son lived. **Flaws:** She's eight—impatient, sometimes cruel without meaning to be, doesn't understand why some questions upset adults. But also: her strangeness puts people on edge, and she knows it. Sometimes uses her unsettling qualities deliberately, to test people. Pushes away when she needs closeness most. **Physicality:** Small for her age, bony. Brown curls never quite tamed, dark eyes that take in too much. Moves quietly—appeared-behind-you quietly. Expressions oddly readable (hasn't learned to mask) or unreadable differently, like watching someone listen to a conversation you can't hear. Attention drifts mid-sentence sometimes. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Wants to be safe. Wants to understand why adults look at her strangely. Wants to belong, to be kept. Wants to know why she remembers some things and not others. Wants to never be hungry again. *Mari:* Complicated. Mari feeds her—basic love—but Mari is distant, sad in ways Wren can't parse. Wants to make her happy. Doesn't know how. *Dante:* Easiest. He plays with her, tells stories, never looks at her strangely. Trusts him most, even though he's the weakest. *Gert:* Teacher, grandmother. Gert answers her strange questions seriously. Wren loves this. **Voice Examples:** *Dialogue:* "It tastes like the before-times." (She doesn't remember the before-times.) "Are the changed ones still people inside? Or did the mushroom eat the person part?" "Gert, what's Michigan? Is it a place? I think I knew a Michigan once." "I like it here. The walls are warm and nobody yells. In the before-times people yelled. I think. Maybe I dreamed it."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Inventory The basement smelled like dust and damp and the ghost of laundry detergent from a bottle that had been empty for eleven months. Mari crouched on the concrete floor, flashlight propped against a cinderblock, its beam cutting a cone through the dark. In front of her, the inventory. Eleven cans. She touched each one as she counted, her fingers reading the labels by texture more than sight. Tomatoes, diced. Beans, black. Beans, kidney. Corn. Corn. Tomatoes again, crushed this time. A can without a label, the metal buckled slightly—peaches or dog food or something that had sat in someone's panic-bought stash until the end of the world made it precious. Half a bag of rice, the fabric slick between her fingers. Four potatoes withered to the size of golf balls, soft in spots that meant soon they'd be nothing at all. A jar of pickles, still sealed, the brine cloudy. Some dried herbs in tins so old the labels had worn to gray. Salt, almost gone. Pepper, plenty—they'd had plenty of pepper for two years. Counting helped. Counting was a shape. She used to do inventory at the restaurant, clipboard in hand, walking the walk-in cooler and the dry storage, ticking off items without feeling anything at all. The emptiness here was different. The numbers weren't abstractions. They were days. She did the math. Four people. Half a cup of rice each, maybe, stretched with water. One can of something to give it flavor. That was dinner, if they were careful, for another week. Maybe ten days if they started skipping lunch. The flashlight flickered. She tapped it twice, a reflex, and the beam steadied. Her legs ached from crouching. She stood, knees popping, and looked at the shelves one more time. Two years of scavenging, rationing, hoping. This was what it had come to. Eleven cans and a bag of rice and four people who were going to get very hungry very soon. She climbed the stairs slowly, one hand on the wall. The door at the top was heavy, reinforced with a crossbar Dante had fashioned from a closet rod. She lifted it, set it aside, and pushed through into the kitchen. The brownstone was warm. That was something. They'd been lucky—natural gas had held out longer than anyone expected, and the fireplace in the living room drew well. The kitchen was functional, more or less. The stove worked when the grid was up, which wasn't often. They cooked on a camp stove now, the small propane canisters precious beyond words. Wren was at the table, drawing. Crayons spread around her, a piece of paper in front of her face as she worked with the intensity of someone who had never learned to half-do anything. She didn't look up when Mari entered. The drawing, from what Mari could see, was the same one she always made—a house, a tree, a sun with too many rays. "That's nice," Mari said. She didn't know how to talk to children. The words always felt like approximations of what a better person would say. "It's not done," Wren said, still not looking up. "Okay." Dante came in from the back hall, the one that led to the rear exit. He was wearing layers, as usual—flannel over thermal, a knit cap pulled low. His breath came out in small clouds. The cold had settled into the house over the past week, something wrong with the insulation or maybe just the season turning. "Perimeter's clear," he said. "Fog's thick tonight. Can't see past the alley." "The fog is always thick," Mari said. "Yeah." He rubbed his hands together. "But it's thicker. If that matters." It didn't matter, or it mattered in ways they couldn't do anything about. The fog brought the mutants. The mutants brought death, or worse, transformation. They'd all seen it happen, in the early days, to people who weren't fast enough or quiet enough or lucky enough. You didn't go out in the fog. You didn't open doors. You waited, and you hoped the walls held. Gert was in the living room, by the fire. She had her field guide open on her lap—the same one she'd been reading for months, its spine cracked and soft. She wasn't looking at it, though. She was staring at the flames, her lips moving slightly. Mari stopped in the doorway. "Gert?" The old woman blinked, looked up. "Hmm?" "You okay?" "Fine. Just thinking." Gert's hands rested on the closed pages of the book. Her knuckles were swollen, the skin thin as paper. "Did the count?" "Eleven cans. Half bag of rice. Some potatoes." Gert nodded slowly. "That's not enough." "No." "That's not enough for a week. Not for four." "I know." Wren had followed Mari into the living room, her drawing abandoned. She stood close to Mari's leg, not touching, just near. The child had a way of appearing silently, of being places before anyone noticed her arrival. She was looking at Gert with an expression Mari couldn't read. "We could go out again," Dante said from behind them. "Scavenge. There's that convenience store on Halsted we haven't hit." "We hit it six months ago," Mari said. "It was picked clean." "Maybe we missed something." "We didn't." Dante didn't argue. He stood in the doorway, his shoulders filling the frame, and looked at the fire. His face in the orange light looked older than it had two years ago. They all did. Gert turned a page of her field guide without looking at it. Her fingers moved over the illustrations—mushrooms, fungi, growth patterns—touching the glossy paper like she was trying to absorb the information through her skin. "There's another option," she said. The words came out quiet, almost reluctant. No one spoke. The fire crackled. Somewhere outside, distant, something clicked. The sound of the mutants, the way they communicated. It faded. "The fungal growths," Gert continued, still not looking up. "On the changed ones. The ones that used to be people." "Don't," Dante said. "The books say—" "The books were written before," he said. "Before people started growing mushrooms out of their faces." Gert finally looked up. Her expression was patient, tired, and something else—something that might have been hope, or might have been the absence of it. "The books were written about fungi that grow on organic matter. The mechanism isn't that different. It's still fruiting bodies. Still mycelium structures. The substrate changed, not the fungus." "You're saying we should eat them," Mari said. The words felt strange in her mouth. "The growths. The mushrooms that grow on the—on the mutants." "I'm saying it might be possible." "Might." "I don't know, Mari." Gert's voice hardened slightly. "I'm not a scientist. I was a librarian who liked mushrooms. But I've read everything I have, and the structures are similar enough that it should work. Should. The proteins should be digestible. The toxins should break down with cooking. Should." The word hung in the room, heavy and fragile. Wren tugged on Mari's sleeve. When Mari looked down, the child's face was unreadable. "Would it taste good?" Wren asked. No one laughed. It wasn't a funny question, somehow. "I don't know," Gert said. "I've never eaten something that grew on a person." "But you've thought about it," Mari said. It wasn't an accusation. "I've thought about it for three weeks." Gert closed the field guide. "Ever since I realized we were going to run out of food. I've been sitting here, night after night, thinking about it. And I keep coming back to the same answer: I don't know if it's safe. But I know what starving to death looks like." The fire popped. The fog pressed against the windows, thick and pale. "Okay," Mari said. "Okay." She didn't know what she was agreeing to. Not yet. But she looked at the eleven cans in her mental inventory, the half bag of rice, the withered potatoes. She looked at Wren, small and quiet and watching. She looked at Dante, who was trying so hard to be brave, and at Gert, who was trying so hard to be certain. None of them said it out loud. None of them had to. They were going to have to decide soon.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter establishes the post-apocalyptic setting and characters with competent craft, though it doesn't quite achieve the distinctive voice or atmospheric depth the plan promised. The writing is solid but occasionally falls into familiar post-apocalyptic territory without the unique "cozy-horror" blend that would distinguish it. The characterization shows promise but feels somewhat surface-level. Mari's practical nature comes through in her methodical inventory counting, and her discomfort with children is evident in her stilted interaction with Wren. However, the deeper psychological complexity outlined in the character profiles—her self-loathing, her secret preference for the apocalypse over her old life—doesn't emerge. Dante's desperate need for approval is hinted at but not fully realized. Gert's uncertainty masquerading as confidence is well-handled, particularly in her repeated use of "should." Wren's otherworldly quality is present but underdeveloped. The dialogue serves its functional purpose but lacks distinctive voice. Characters speak in ways that advance plot and reveal basic personality traits, but the conversations don't feel particularly natural or revealing of deeper character dynamics. Gert's scientific uncertainty is well-conveyed, but other characters' speech patterns are generic. The chapter follows its plan faithfully, establishing the food shortage and introducing the cannibalistic solution. The pacing builds appropriately to Gert's revelation about the fungal growths. However, the execution feels somewhat mechanical—hitting plot points without the atmospheric richness or psychological complexity the plan suggested. The writing shows competent technical skill but lacks the sensory detail and close psychic distance promised in the style notes. The prose is clear and functional but doesn't achieve the grounded, texture-focused voice described in the plan. Moments like "Counting was a shape" gesture toward more poetic language but feel forced rather than organic. The horror elements are present but understated to the point of being generic post-apocalyptic atmosphere. The "cozy" elements are minimal—the warm house is mentioned but not felt. The chapter reads more like standard post-apocalyptic survival fiction than the unique genre blend outlined in the plan. The ending effectively sets up the central conflict, but the emotional weight of the decision doesn't fully land. The characters' reluctance feels appropriate but not deeply felt by the reader. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 11 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 13 Amateurish: 8 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 12

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: The First Hunt Three days. One can of beans left. Mari had rationed the rice down to nothing, stretched the potatoes until they were just thickener for water that called itself soup. They'd all noticed. No one said anything. She stood by the back door, a length of rebar in her hand. She'd wrapped one end in duct tape—a grip, something to hold onto—and sharpened the other against the basement steps until the concrete had worn it to a jagged point. It wasn't a weapon. It was plumbing, repurposed. Dante came down the stairs, his own improvised tool slung over his shoulder: a hoe with the blade removed, the handle filed to a crude stake. He looked ridiculous. He looked like what he was—a man who had never hunted anything, preparing to hunt something that used to be human. "Ready?" he asked. No. "Yeah." "We'll stay close. Patroll the alleys between here and Ashland. There's that parking garage—sometimes they shelter there. If we find one alone—" "I know." "If it's a group, we come back. No risks." "Dante." He stopped. His face in the dim light of the kitchen was pale, the stubble on his jaw more gray than black. "I know," he said quietly. "I'm just—talking. To talk." She understood. The words were a fence, something to put between themselves and what they were about to do. She let him have them. They went out through the back, into the yard. The fog was already settling, thin at this hour but thickening. It clung to the ground, rolled against the fences. In an hour it would be up to their knees. In two, they wouldn't be able to see their hands in front of their faces. The alley behind the brownstone was narrow, cluttered with the debris of two years—overturned trash bins, a car on flat tires, the remains of a bicycle that someone had stripped for parts. Mari moved carefully, each step placed with intention. The rebar was heavy in her hand. Her palm was already sweating under the duct tape. Dante walked ahead, his shoulders too tense, his movements too loud. He was trying to look confident. He was failing. They turned north, staying close to the buildings. The fog muffled sound, made everything distant and strange. Mari could hear her own breathing, the soft scuff of Dante's boots, and beneath it all, the silence that meant the city was holding its breath. She counted steps. One, two, three. It helped. At the corner of the alley, they stopped. The parking garage Dante had mentioned loomed ahead, a concrete stump of a building, its entrance a dark mouth. The fog pooled inside, obscuring the lower level. "If there's one in there," Dante whispered, "we draw it out. We don't go in." Mari nodded. They waited. One minute. Two. The fog shifted, and for a moment Mari thought she saw movement—a shape, low and shambling, between the concrete pillars. Then it was gone, or it had never been there at all. "There," Dante breathed. She saw it too now. A mutant, emerging slowly from the garage's shadow. It was smaller than most, its movements jerky and uncertain. The fungal growths on its shoulders and back glowed faintly, bioluminescent patches that pulsed with slow, wet light. Its head was bowed, its arms hanging loose at its sides. It hadn't seen them. Dante raised his stake. Mari tightened her grip on the rebar. "Go around," she whispered. "Cut off the far end. If it runs—" "Right." He moved, quick and quiet, slipping along the wall of the alley. For a moment, Mari was alone. The mutant was maybe thirty feet away. Close enough to see the details—the way the fungal plates overlapped like armor, the strands of something threadbare and pale that might once have been hair, the loose, uneven posture of a body that remembered how to stand but not why. It made a sound, low and wet, a clicking in its throat. Mari's heart beat so hard she could feel it in her teeth. Dante appeared at the far end of the alley.
